question: What is the main advantage of using generics over raw types in Java?
options: Generics run faster than raw types at runtime
Generics provide compile-time type safety, eliminating the need for manual casting
Generics allow a class to have multiple constructors
Generics prevent all runtime exceptions
explanation: Generics move type checking to compile time. With raw types, list.get(0) returns Object and requires a manual cast like (String) — which can fail at runtime with ClassCastException. With List<String>, get(0) returns String directly, enforced by the compiler.

question: What does List<String> list = new ArrayList<>() achieve compared to List list = new ArrayList()?
options: They are identical — generics are only a naming convention
The generic version is type-safe; the raw type version requires manual casting and is not type-safe
The generic version uses more memory
The raw type version is faster to compile
explanation: List<String> constrains the list to Strings at compile time. list.get(0) returns String directly — no cast. The raw List version returns Object, requiring (String) list.get(0) which can throw ClassCastException if the wrong type was stored.

question:
Given: public class Box<T> { private T value; public T getValue() { return value; } }
What does Box<Integer> intBox = new Box<>(42) do?
options: Creates a Box that can hold any type, and 42 is stored as Object
Sets T to Integer for this instance; getValue() will return Integer without casting
Causes a compile error because 42 is a primitive, not an object
Creates a Box of type T which must be cast to Integer before use
explanation: When you write Box<Integer>, T is resolved to Integer throughout that instance. Java auto-boxes the primitive 42 to Integer. getValue() returns Integer — no casting required, fully type-safe.

question: What does '<T extends Number>' mean in a method signature?
options: T must be exactly the Number class, nothing else
T can be any type that is a subclass of Number (e.g. Integer, Double, Float)
T can be any type, but it will be cast to Number at runtime
The method can only be called with T set to the Number class
explanation: Bounded generics restrict T to Number and its subclasses. This allows calling Number methods (like doubleValue()) on T safely. Types like Integer, Double, and Float all extend Number and are valid.

question: Why is the keyword 'final' used on a Helper Class like 'public final class MathHelper'?
options: To make all its methods run faster
To prevent the class from being instantiated
To prevent the class from being inherited (subclassed)
To make all its fields constants
explanation: 'final' on a class prevents inheritance — no other class can extend MathHelper. This is appropriate because helper classes are stateless utility toolboxes with no reason to be subclassed. To prevent instantiation, the private constructor is used.

question: How is a static method in a Helper Class called, assuming MathHelper is imported?
options: new MathHelper().add(5, 10)
MathHelper helper = new MathHelper(); helper.add(5, 10);
MathHelper.add(5, 10)
super.MathHelper.add(5, 10)
explanation: Static methods are called via the class name directly, without creating an instance: MathHelper.add(5, 10). This is possible because static methods belong to the class, not to any object instance.

question: What does 'GoF' stand for in the context of design patterns?
options: Group of Functions
Gang of Four — the four authors of 'Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software' (1994)
General Object Framework
Generic Object Foundation
explanation: GoF = Gang of Four: Erich Gamma, Richard Helm, Ralph Johnson, and John Vlissides. Their 1994 book catalogued 23 foundational OO design patterns and remains the standard reference.

question: What is the core guarantee of the Singleton design pattern?
options: Only one method can be called at a time on the object
Only one instance of the class is ever created, with a global access point to it
The class can only have one field
The class can only be used in one package
explanation: Singleton ensures exactly one instance exists globally. It uses a private constructor (preventing external instantiation) and a static getInstance() method (the global access point) to enforce this.

options: Three separate EngineCore objects are created
The first call creates the object; the 2nd and 3rd calls return the same existing object
A compile error occurs because getInstance() is static
The constructor is called 3 times but only 1 object is stored
explanation: First call: instance == null, so 'instance = new EngineCore()' runs, creating the object. Second and third calls: instance != null, so the if block is skipped and the existing instance is returned. The constructor is called exactly once.

question: Which principle does the Singleton pattern violate?
options: Open/Closed Principle
Liskov Substitution Principle
Single Responsibility Principle
Interface Segregation Principle
explanation: The Singleton class takes on TWO responsibilities: its core functionality AND managing its own instantiation. The Single Responsibility Principle states a class should have only one reason to change.

question: What does the Factory Pattern hide from the caller?
options: The method names of the created object
The 'new' call — the exact concrete class used to create the object
The package location of the created class
The return type of the created object
explanation: The Factory Pattern hides the 'new SpecificClass()' call. The caller asks the factory for an object by type ('SQUARE'), and the factory decides which concrete class to instantiate. The caller only knows the interface (Shape).

question:
In the ShapeFactory example, what type is 'shape1' stored as?
Shape shape1 = shapeFactory.getShape("SQUARE");
options: Square — the exact concrete type
ShapeFactory — the factory type
Shape — the interface type
Object — the base Java type
explanation: getShape() returns type Shape (the interface). Even though the actual object is a Square, it is stored as Shape. This is the 'slight limitation' noted in the lecture — only Shape interface methods are accessible directly.

question: How does using a Factory Pattern in a game class reduce coupling when creating Player, Enemy, Bullet, Wall, and Tree?
options: The game class imports fewer packages
The game class only associates with the factory, removing direct 'new' calls for each entity type — adding new entities only changes the factory, not the game class
The game class becomes abstract
The game class no longer needs to import entity classes
explanation: Without a factory, the game class directly instantiates every entity — creating tight coupling. With a factory, the game class only knows about the factory. New entity types are handled by adding them to the factory, following the Open/Closed Principle.

question: How is Abstract Factory different from Factory Pattern?
options: Abstract Factory is used for creating a single object; Factory is for creating multiple objects
Abstract Factory creates families of related objects across multiple factories; Factory creates objects from one product type
Abstract Factory uses inheritance; Factory uses composition
They are the same — 'abstract factory' just means the factory is in an abstract class
explanation: Factory Pattern hides construction of ONE product family (e.g. all shapes from ShapeFactory). Abstract Factory is a 'factory of factories' — it groups multiple factories with a common theme (ShapeFactory + RoundedShapeFactory under AbstractFactory).

options: RoundedRectangle — because false means rounded is disabled
Rectangle — because getFactory(false) returns ShapeFactory (non-rounded), which creates a normal Rectangle
Square — because false is the default
An error — getShape needs the boolean parameter too
explanation: getFactory(false) returns a ShapeFactory (not rounded). shapeFactory.getShape("RECTANGLE") on ShapeFactory creates a normal Rectangle. getFactory(true) would return RoundedShapeFactory, producing a RoundedRectangle.

question: In the Abstract Factory architecture, what is the relationship between ShapeFactory, RoundedShapeFactory, and AbstractFactory?
options: ShapeFactory and RoundedShapeFactory ARE AbstractFactory (they extend it)
AbstractFactory extends ShapeFactory
They are unrelated classes connected only through FactoryProducer
ShapeFactory and RoundedShapeFactory compose AbstractFactory
explanation: Both ShapeFactory and RoundedShapeFactory extend AbstractFactory. FactoryProducer returns either one as an AbstractFactory reference. The diagram shows 'extends' arrows from both concrete factories to AbstractFactory.

question: In the Factory Registry pattern, what does entityManager.registerFactory(Player.class, new PlayerFactory()) do?
options: It creates a Player object immediately
It maps the Player class type to its PlayerFactory, stored in the entity manager (e.g. in a HashMap) for later use
It deletes the PlayerFactory after use
It makes PlayerFactory the default factory for all entity types
explanation: registerFactory stores the mapping from Player.class → PlayerFactory in a registry (typically a HashMap<Class, Factory>). When createEntity(Player.class) is later called, the manager looks up the registered factory and uses it to create the object.

question: In this module's two-layer architecture, which layer contains 'Player', 'Orc', and 'Bullet'?
options: The Abstract Engine Layer
The Application (Game) Layer
The Platform Independence Layer
The Resource Management Layer
explanation: Player, Orc, and Bullet are concrete, game-specific entities — they belong in the Application (Game) Layer. The Engine Layer contains abstract base classes like Entity, Collidable, and NonCollidable that are game-agnostic.

question: Which statement correctly describes the relationship between layers and components?
options: A component can span multiple layers
A layer contains exactly one component
A layer can have many components; components are grouped based on shared functionality
Components and layers are the same concept
explanation: The lecture states: 'A layer can have many components' and 'Components are usually grouped based on shared functionality (like entities!)'. A layer is a horizontal architectural grouping; components are functional units within a layer.

question: In the entity hierarchy diagram, what does the dashed line between the two layers represent?
options: An inheritance relationship
A composition relationship
The boundary separating the Game Engine Layer (above) from the Game Layer (below)
A network connection between components
explanation: The dashed line visually separates the Abstract Engine Layer (Entity, Collidable, NonCollidable) from the Application/Game Layer (GameX Collidable, GameX NonCollidable, Player, Orc, Bullet, Moon, Tree).

question: What is the primary role of a Manager Class in entity management?
options: To do all the work for each entity, including movement and collision logic
To oversee a list of objects, invoke their updates, and ask them to execute methods — without doing the work itself
To create all entity objects using 'new' directly
To store only one entity at a time
explanation: The manager is an orchestrator, not a worker. It holds a list of entities, calls update() on them, and triggers methods like reactToCollision(). Each entity does its own work — the manager just coordinates.

question: Why is storing entities in a List<iCollidable> better than a List<Object> for collision management?
options: List<iCollidable> uses less memory
List<iCollidable> allows calling interface methods (collidesWith, reactToCollision) directly without casting, enabling polymorphic collision handling
List<Object> cannot store entity objects
List<iCollidable> automatically detects collisions
explanation: With List<iCollidable>, you can call any method declared in iCollidable (like reactToCollision()) directly on each element. With List<Object>, you would need to cast to iCollidable first. Polymorphism then ensures each entity's override runs.

question: Player detects a collision with an EnemyBullet. Using the iCollidable interface and polymorphism, what happens?
options: Both Player and EnemyBullet execute the same default collision behaviour
Player's reactToCollision() takes damage; EnemyBullet's reactToCollision() plays explosion animation and deletes itself — each handles it their own way
The manager class directly removes the EnemyBullet and reduces Player health
Nothing happens until the game manually checks for collision responses
explanation: This is polymorphism in action. Both Player and EnemyBullet implement iCollidable and override reactToCollision() with their own behaviour. The collision manager calls reactToCollision() on both, each executing their own logic.

question: In the object management context, what does 'code to abstraction' mean and why is it recommended?
options: Write all code using abstract classes to prevent instantiation
Write code that depends on abstract types or interfaces rather than concrete classes, so it works with any subtype without modification
Avoid using concrete classes entirely in a game
Only use abstract methods in all classes
explanation: Coding to abstraction means your manager stores List<iCollidable> rather than List<Player> or List<Bullet>. This means any new entity type that implements iCollidable works automatically with the existing manager — no changes needed.

question: Which OOP concept allows a single collision detection loop to handle Player, Bullet, Enemy, and PowerUp all differently, despite being stored in the same list?
options: Encapsulation
Inheritance alone
Polymorphism — each entity overrides reactToCollision() with its own implementation
Abstraction alone
explanation: Polymorphism + method overriding: when the loop calls entity.reactToCollision(), Java resolves which override to call based on the actual runtime type. Player runs its damage logic, Bullet runs its destroy logic — all from one loop.

question: A student writes: 'EngineCore e1 = EngineCore.getInstance(); EngineCore e2 = EngineCore.getInstance();'. What is the result?
options: e1 and e2 are two different EngineCore objects
A compile error — getInstance() cannot be called twice
e1 and e2 point to the same single EngineCore object
e1 holds the real object; e2 holds a copy
explanation: The first call creates the object and stores it in the static 'instance' field. The second call finds instance != null and returns the same object. e1 == e2 would be true — they reference the exact same object in memory.

question: A student writes: 'Square shape1 = shapeFactory.getShape("SQUARE");'. Will this compile?
options: Yes — getShape returns the exact concrete type
No — getShape() returns type Shape (the interface), which cannot be assigned to Square without casting
Yes — because Square implements Shape
No — because shapeFactory is not declared as Square
explanation: getShape() declares its return type as Shape, not Square. Even though the actual object is a Square, you can only assign it to a Shape reference without explicit casting. This is the 'slight limitation' mentioned in the lecture — you must store the result in the parent/interface type.

question: Why is the Abstract Engine designed to be game-agnostic (not tied to any specific game)?
options: So it runs faster without game-specific code
So the same engine can be reused to build multiple different games or simulations — the engine is the reusable foundation, not game logic
Because game-specific code would cause compile errors in the engine
Because the engine is always written first before the game concept is known
explanation: A game-agnostic engine (with abstract Entity, Collidable, physics, input, etc.) can power any game that extends and implements its abstract classes. The lecture explicitly states the engine should be 'robust enough to do more than just one specific thing'.

question:
Given: public <T> T getFirst(List<T> list) { return list.get(0); }
This method is in a non-generic class. What is true about this method?
options: It will not compile because T is not defined at the class level
The <T> before the return type makes this a generic method — T is scoped to this method only
T defaults to Object because no class-level type is defined
The method can only be called if the class is also declared generic
explanation: A method can declare its own type parameter independently with <T> before the return type. T is scoped to just this method — it does not need a class-level type parameter. The compiler infers T from the argument passed when the method is called.

question: The lecture mentions 'don't get overly upset about down-casting for this module, but try to avoid it by design.' What design approach helps avoid down-casting?
options: Using more concrete types in lists
Using interface or abstract type lists (e.g. List<iCollidable>) so methods can be called polymorphically without needing to cast to the concrete type
Using static methods instead of instance methods
Creating separate lists for each entity type
explanation: Down-casting (casting from a base type to a specific subtype) is often a sign of poor design. Using interface lists (List<iCollidable>) avoids it — you call the interface method, and polymorphism routes to the correct override without ever needing to know the concrete type.
